title: SEGMENT 4 — The matching problem: HLA and the immunological wall

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — The matching problem: HLA and the immunological wall
Let's stop and look in detail at the immunological problem that almost killed the field in its infancy, because understanding it is essential to understanding how modern transplant works.
The problem is called HLA matching, and it is the central challenge of allogeneic transplantation — the kind of transplant where the donor cells come from a different person.
Here's the analogy.
Imagine that every cell in your body wears a uniform — a distinctive set of patches on its surface that tells your immune system "I belong to this team.
" The patches are protein molecules, encoded by a cluster of genes on chromosome 6 called the major histocompatibility complex, or MHC.
In humans, we usually call them by their alternative name, HLA, the human leukocyte antigens.
The two names refer to the same molecules.
HLA is just the human-specific term.
There are two big classes of HLA.
Class I is on essentially every cell in your body.
It includes the loci called HLA-A, HLA-B, and HLA-C.
Class II is mostly on professional immune cells — dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells, activated T cells — and includes HLA-DR, HLA-DQ, and HLA-DP.
Each of these loci has dozens to hundreds of alleles in the human population — variant sequences that differ from one person to the next.
The combinatorics are staggering.

Your particular set of HLA alleles, inherited from your mother and father, is essentially a unique molecular fingerprint, shared with very few other people on earth — unless you have a sibling who, by chance, inherited the same set from your shared parents.
The biological purpose of HLA is to display chunks of protein — peptides — on the cell surface, where T cells can inspect them.
Class I HLA displays peptides from inside the cell, including viral peptides if the cell is infected.
Class II displays peptides from things the cell has engulfed from outside.
T cells walk up, sample the displayed peptides, and decide whether anything looks dangerous.
This is the foundation of adaptive immunity.
But here's the catch.
T cells are trained, during their education in the thymus, to recognize peptides displayed on your own HLA as "self.
" Anyone else's HLA, with a slightly different shape, looks foreign — almost regardless of what peptide is being displayed.
The HLA itself is the immune target.
The shape of the molecule is the "team uniform," and uniforms from other teams trigger attack.
So now imagine you're transplanting bone marrow from one person to another.
Two immunological wars can break out.
War one: host-versus-graft.
The recipient's residual immune system sees the donor cells, recognizes their HLA as foreign, and attacks them.
This is conventional graft rejection — the same reason a kidney transplant from an unmatched donor gets rejected.
In bone marrow transplant, this typically results in graft failure — the donor stem cells get killed before they can establish residence, and the patient is left with no functioning bone marrow.
Fatal, unless rescued by another transplant or by autologous backup cells.
War two: graft-versus-host.
This is the unique problem of bone marrow transplant.
Because what you're transplanting includes a fully functional donor immune system — T cells, primarily — those donor T cells will see the recipient's tissues, recognize them as foreign, and attack.
This is graft-versus-host disease, or GVHD, and we'll spend a whole segment on it.
It is the central chronic complication of allogeneic HSCT and the reason matching matters so much.
So HLA matching reduces both wars.
The closer the match between donor HLA and recipient HLA, the less the recipient's immune system attacks the graft, and the less the donor's immune system attacks the host.
In practice, the field looks at typically ten HLA loci — two alleles each at HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C, HLA-DRB1, and HLA-DQB1 — and counts matches out of ten.
A 10/10 match is the gold standard.
9/10 is acceptable.
Less than that gets risky fast.
And the matching has to be at high resolution, at the DNA sequence level, because even single-amino-acid mismatches in the right position can trigger massive immune reactions.
The hardest part of HLA matching is the math.
You inherit one copy of the whole HLA cluster — called a haplotype — from each parent.

Since HLA is essentially co-inherited as a unit, with very rare recombination within the cluster, you and a sibling have a 25 percent chance of being a full HLA match, a 50 percent chance of being half-matched (sharing one haplotype), and a 25 percent chance of being completely unmatched.
So if you have one sibling, you have a 25 percent chance they're a perfect donor.
If you have three siblings, your odds rise to roughly 58 percent that at least one is a match.
Most patients, especially in small families, do not have a matched sibling, which has historically been the central access problem of HSCT.

For patients without a sibling donor, you turn to unrelated donor registries — institutions like the National Marrow Donor Program in the US, operating the Be The Match registry, which has on the order of forty million potential donors registered globally.
The chance of finding a 10/10 unrelated match depends heavily on ancestry.
For patients of Northern European descent, the registry hit rate is around 75 percent.

For patients of African, Asian, Hispanic, or mixed ancestry, the hit rate is dramatically lower — sometimes under 25 percent — because the registries are dominated by donors of European ancestry, and HLA diversity is much higher in people whose ancestors evolved in Africa.

This inequity in access is one of the central ongoing problems of transplant medicine, and it is the central motivator for two of the modern innovations we'll cover after the break: cord blood transplant, which has looser matching requirements, and haploidentical transplant, which uses half-matched family donors and has opened HSCT to essentially every patient with a living relative.
These are not just clinical advances.
They are equity advances.
They take a therapy that, for decades, worked best for white European patients with large families, and extend it to populations that the registries failed.
So when you read about HSCT, anywhere, the HLA matching problem is the deep structural issue underneath every clinical decision.
It determines who can be transplanted, how well they do, what side effects they suffer, and — crucially — whether the transplant is even available to them at all.
We'll come back to it.

For now, hold the picture: every cell has team uniforms; transplant works only when the uniforms more or less match; mismatching causes immune wars in both directions; and the registries, until very recently, made the procedure dramatically more accessible to some populations than others.
